message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://www.codesota.com/text-to-speech
**Title:** Text-to-Speech Models 2026: TTS Leaderboard, Kokoro, XTTS and Voice Agents (CodeSOTA)
**Found via:** broad/primary — model landscape & naturalness vs Kokoro search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.
- cloudflare-one: Guides Cloudflare One Zero Trust and SASE work across Access, Gateway, WARP, Tunnel, Cloudflare WAN, DLP, CASB, device posture, and identity. Use when designing, configuring, troubleshooting, or reviewing Cloudflare One deployments. Retrieval-first: use current Cloudflare docs/API schemas instead of embedded product docs.
- cloudflare-one-migrations: Plans migrations from Zscaler ZIA/ZPA, Palo Alto, legacy VPN, SWG, or SASE stacks to Cloudflare One. Use for migration assessments, policy mapping, rollout plans, and parity/gap analysis.
- durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- sandbox-sdk: Build sandboxed applications for secure code execution. Load when building AI code execution, code interpreters, CI/CD systems, interactive dev environments, or executing untrusted code. Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- turnstile-spin: Set up Cloudflare Turnstile end-to-end in a project — scan the codebase, create the widget via the Cloudflare API, deploy the managed siteverify Worker, write the frontend snippets, validate, and persist the skill. Load this when a user asks to add Turnstile, set up CAPTCHA, protect a form from bots, or fix a Turnstile integration. Mirrors developers.cloudflare.com/turnstile/spin.
- web-perf: Analyzes web performance using Chrome DevTools MCP. Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.
- workers-best-practices: Reviews and authors Cloudflare Workers code against production best practices. Load when writing new Workers, reviewing Worker code, configuring wrangler.jsonc, or checking for common Workers anti-patterns (streaming, floating promises, global state, secrets, bindings, observability). Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the page content to extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract all factual claims about open-source/local text-to-speech (TTS) models relevant to: naturalness/quality benchmarks (MOS, leaderboard rankings, win rates), Kokoro vs other models (XTTS-v2, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Zonos, etc.), model licenses, model sizes/parameters, audio sample rates, voice cloning, multi-voice support, word/phoneme timestamps/alignment, inference speed/real-time factor, RAM/VRAM needs, Apple Silicon/MPS/ONNX/CoreML support. For each claim, quote the exact text. Also report: the publish date or last-updated date of the page, whether this is a benchmark leaderboard or marketing/blog, and how rankings are determined (e.g., human votes, MOS scores). List the specific model rankings if a leaderboard is present.

content.content:
# Text-to-Speech Models 2026: Factual Claims Extract

**Page Status:** CodeSOTA TTS Registry & Research (Updated 2026-06-15) | Benchmark leaderboard with measured runs + reported registry tier

---

## Naturalness/Quality Benchmarks

**MOS Rankings (Literature-Sourced):**
- Top performers clustered at 4.7–4.8: "ElevenLabs Turbo v2.5" (4.8), "Sesame CSM" (4.7), "OpenAI TTS HD" (4.7)
- Kokoro tied commercially on independent eval: "Tied with commercial APIs on CodeSOTA's independent UTMOS eval (4.48)"
- Gap characterization: "the quality gap is nearly closed: the best open-source TTS (Sesame CSM, 4.7 MOS) is within 0.1–0.3"

**UTMOS Measured:**
- Gradium: "4.412 UTMOS on 50 Harvard sentences"
- Kokoro: Measured independent run noted but specific UTMOS score embedded in "4.48" claim above

**Ranking Methodology:** "MOS stays visible, but it is not ranked as equivalent to CodeSOTA-measured runs. Sub-0.1 MOS gaps are marked as noise."

---

## Kokoro vs. Competitors

**Kokoro v1.0 Profile:**
- License: "Apache-2.0"
- Parameters: "82M params"
- Speed: "~10x real-time on CPU"
- MOS: 4.5 (reported), 4.48 UTMOS (measured)
- Use case: "Apache / MIT license, <1B params, CPU-friendly"

**Direct Comparisons:**
- vs. XTTS v2: "XTTS v2 covers 17 languages" (implies Kokoro does not); XTTS v2 MOS 4.5
- vs. Piper: "3.6 reported MOS" (Piper significantly lower)
- vs. F5-TTS: "4.4 reported MOS" on F5-TTS
- vs. Fish Speech 1.5: "4.4 reported MOS"

**Kokoro as "negative control":** "Kokoro is included as a useful negative control: it exposes speed and text recipes, not native emotion conditioning." (Lacks direct emotion vector controls)

---

## Model Parameters & Specifications

| Model | Params | License/Type | Architecture |
|-------|--------|--------------|--------------|
| Kokoro v1.0 | 82M | Apache-2.0 | "Lightweight autoregressive" |
| XTTS v2 | 467M | Open Source | "GPT-like + VITS decoder" |
| F5-TTS | 335M | Open Source | "Flow-matching (non-autoregressive)" |
| Parler-TTS | 880M | Open Source | "Prompt-controlled Transformer" |
| Fish Speech 1.5 | 500M | Open Source | "VQGAN + Transformer" |
| Orpheus TTS | 3B | Open Source | "LLM-based (Llama backbone)" |
| Dia 1.6B | 1.6B | Open Source | "Transformer + non-verbal tokens" |
| Sesame CSM | 1B+ | Open Source | "Conversational Speech Model" |

---

## Voice Cloning & Features

- F5-TTS: "F5-TTS uses flow-matching for fast cloning"
- XTTS v2: "Clone from seconds of reference audio"
- Multilingual capability (XTTS v2): "17 languages"

---

## Inference Speed / Real-Time Factor

- Kokoro: "~10x real-time on CPU"
- Cartesia Sonic 2: "<90ms TTFB"
- ElevenLabs Flash v2.5: "~120ms" TTFB
- Gradium: "299 ms p95 first-byte latency"

---

## Intelligibility (Hard Text)

- Gradium: "13.4% normalized WER" (best run on 30-prompt suite)
- Measurement method: "WER, CER, entity accuracy, and latency for production TTS"

---

## Emotion/Expressiveness Features

**Sesame CSM:** "Handles emotion, laughter, pauses, multi-speaker"

**Kokoro Proxy Controls:** "Kokoro proxy controls" tested for tempo/pause (0.688 baseline), but lacks native emotion vector

**Zonos (Research Model):** "8D emotion vector, rate, pitch std, speaker embedding" controls

---

## Apple Silicon / Edge Deployment

- Piper: "ONNX Runtime local inference" (ONNX format supported)
- Kokoro: Confirmed "CPU-friendly" but MPS/CoreML support not explicitly stated

---

## Limitations & Caveats

"MOS is one axis... The best MOS can lose to a worse-MOS model that ships a better voice library and sub-200ms TTFB."

Sample count disclosure required: "no CI yet; measured run exposes sample count and artifacts"

Human MOS reference range: "Reference recordings of real speech typically score 4.5–4.7 — the ceiling."

---

## Not Covered in Page

Audio sample rates, word/phoneme timestamps, RAM/VRAM requirements, and StyleTTS2/MeloTTS/Chatterbox detailed specs are **not provided**.

content.text:
I have the extracted content. Let me assess the source and structure the key claims.

The page is CodeSOTA, a TTS registry/leaderboard updated 2026-06-15, combining measured runs (UTMOS) with literature-sourced MOS. It mixes independent benchmarking with reported registry data. This places it between primary benchmark and secondary aggregation — I'll rate it secondary since it aggregates literature MOS heavily and the "measured" methodology is lightly described (no CI, sample-count caveats).

claims.claim: Kokoro v1.0 is an 82M-parameter, Apache-2.0 licensed model that runs ~10x real-time on CPU and scored 4.48 on CodeSOTA's independent UTMOS evaluation, tying it with commercial APIs.
claims.quote: Tied with commercial APIs on CodeSOTA's independent UTMOS eval (4.48)

claims.claim: The open-source-vs-commercial quality gap is nearly closed; the best open-source TTS (Sesame CSM at 4.7 MOS) is within 0.1-0.3 MOS of top commercial models like ElevenLabs Turbo v2.5 (4.8) and OpenAI TTS HD (4.7).
claims.quote: the quality gap is nearly closed: the best open-source TTS (Sesame CSM, 4.7 MOS) is within 0.1–0.3

claims.claim: Among open models, Kokoro's reported MOS (~4.5) is roughly tied with XTTS v2 (4.5), above F5-TTS and Fish Speech 1.5 (both 4.4 reported MOS), and well above Piper (3.6 reported MOS).
claims.quote: vs. Piper: "3.6 reported MOS" (Piper significantly lower); vs. F5-TTS: "4.4 reported MOS"; vs. Fish Speech 1.5: "4.4 reported MOS"

claims.claim: Kokoro exposes only speed and text recipes, not native emotion conditioning, making it a 'negative control' versus models with explicit emotion controls such as Zonos (8D emotion vector, rate, pitch std, speaker embedding) and Sesame CSM (emotion, laughter, pauses, multi-speaker).
claims.quote: Kokoro is included as a useful negative control: it exposes speed and text recipes, not native emotion conditioning.

claims.claim: XTTS v2 (467M params) supports 17 languages and can clone a voice from seconds of reference audio, while F5-TTS (335M, flow-matching non-autoregressive) is positioned for fast cloning.
claims.quote: XTTS v2 covers 17 languages ... Clone from seconds of reference audio ... F5-TTS uses flow-matching for fast cloning

content.text:
I extracted the key claims from the CodeSOTA TTS leaderboard page.

Summary of findings:
- Source: CodeSOTA "Text-to-Speech Models 2026" registry/leaderboard, last updated 2026-06-15. Rated secondary — it blends a few independently measured UTMOS runs with heavily literature-sourced MOS scores, and self-discloses methodology limits ("no CI yet," sub-0.1 MOS gaps marked as noise).
- Most relevant to the research question: Kokoro is profiled as 82M params, Apache-2.0, ~10x real-time on CPU, with a measured UTMOS of 4.48 tying commercial APIs. The page frames the open-vs-commercial gap as nearly closed (Sesame CSM 4.7 within 0.1-0.3 of ElevenLabs/OpenAI).
- For "beating Kokoro on naturalness": the page's reported MOS figures put XTTS v2 (~4.5) on par with Kokoro, F5-TTS and Fish Speech 1.5 slightly below (4.4), and Piper well below (3.6). It does not give Kokoro a clear naturalness loser among small open models.
- Gaps for this research question: the page does NOT cover word/phoneme timestamps/alignment, audio sample rates, RAM/VRAM, or MPS/CoreML support, and only confirms Piper (ONNX) and Kokoro (CPU-friendly) for edge deployment. StyleTTS2, MeloTTS, Chatterbox, Orpheus, and Kitten-TTS details are largely absent or thin.

Caveat: most MOS numbers are labeled "reported" (literature-sourced vendor/paper claims), not independent evaluation — only the UTMOS figures are described as CodeSOTA-measured.
